description: Emergency situations, health, and asking for help

items.notes: Partitive form of 'apu' (help). Shouted to attract attention in an emergency. The most critical single word to know.

The Finnish emergency number is 112 (EU standard). 'Soittakaa' is the plural/formal imperative of 'soittaa' — used to address multiple people or one person formally in urgent situations. 'Hätänumeroon' is illative case (indicating direction/target of the call).
'Lääkäriä' is the partitive form of 'lääkäri'. The verb 'tarvita' takes the partitive case for its object.
'Soittakaa' is the plural/formal imperative of 'soittaa'. Used when urgently requesting bystanders to call for help. In Finland, call 112 for all emergencies.
'Lähin' is the superlative of 'lähellä' (near). Finnish public hospitals have 24h emergency rooms (päivystys).
Point to the area while saying this. Finnish expresses 'having' with the adessive case: 'minulla' (on me) + 'on' (is). Useful at a doctor's office or emergency room.
Basic statement of illness. 'Sairas' is an adjective meaning sick/ill. Can be followed by specifics about symptoms.
'Kuumetta' is the partitive form of 'kuume' (fever). The partitive is used because fever is an uncountable/ongoing condition. Normal body temperature in Finland is measured in Celsius (37°C).
'Lääkettä' is the partitive form of 'lääke'. In Finland, pharmacies ('apteekki') are separate from shops — many medicines require a prescription ('resepti').
Follow with the allergen in the allative case (-lle), e.g., 'Olen allerginen pähkinöille' (I am allergic to nuts), 'Olen allerginen maitotuotteille' (I am allergic to dairy products).
Critical emergency phrase. 'En' is the first person negative verb. 'Voi' is from 'voida' (can/to be able). 'Hengittää' means to breathe. Call 112 immediately.
Plural/formal imperative of 'varoa' (to beware/be careful). Used to warn people of immediate danger. Singular informal form is 'Varo!'.
Compound word from 'tuli' (fire) + 'palo' (burn/blaze). Shout this to alert others. Finnish fire department number is 112 (same number for all emergencies).
'Soittakaa' is the plural/formal imperative of 'soittaa'. The non-emergency police number in Finland is 0295 419 800, but for emergencies always call 112.
Passive perfect construction. 'Minua' is the partitive of 'minä'. 'Ryöstetty' is the past participle of 'ryöstää' (to rob). Report to police at the nearest station or call 112 if in danger.
Perfect tense: 'olen' + past participle 'kadottanut'. The possessive suffix '-ni' on 'lompakko' means 'my'. Report lost items to police or local lost-and-found ('löytötavarat').

'Tulkkia' is the partitive form of 'tulkki' (interpreter). In Finland, public services (hospitals, police) are often required to provide interpreter services. You can also say 'Tarvitsen englanninkielistä tulkkia' (I need an English-speaking interpreter).
112 is the universal emergency number across the EU, including Finland. It connects to police, fire, and medical services. Operators often speak English. This number works even without a SIM card.
